transcript.text: Design a chat application like WhatsApp or Messenger: 1:1 and group messaging, online presence, delivery and read receipts, message history, and push notifications when offline.

blocks.md:
# Design a Chat Application (WhatsApp / Messenger)

> Core tension in one line: a chat system isn't a request/response service — it's a **stateful push system** where the hard part is *finding the recipient's live connection and getting the message there in order*, not storing the message. The whole design hangs on the persistent-connection fan-out.

---

## 1. Scope, Requirements & Assumptions

> 🎙️ "Here's how I'll structure this: I'll lock scope with a couple of quick assumptions, state the requirements and rough scale, sketch the API and core entities, then draw the high-level design one flow at a time — send a 1:1 message, group fan-out, presence, receipts, offline push — and then go deep on the two or three hardest parts: the connection fan-out, message ordering, and the celebrity/large-group problem. I'll check in with you as I go. Sound good?"

I'll work backwards from the customer: a user opens the app and expects every message they send to land on every recipient's device **in order, within a second, even if the recipient is offline right now.** Cost and operations are first-class — at this scale the fan-out fleet is the budget, so I'll keep flagging the $ and the on-call surface.

### 🔒 Scope lock (stated as vetoable assumptions — stop me if any is wrong)

These are the answers that **fork the architecture**. I'll state each as an assumption and move:

1. **Scale: WhatsApp-class, ~500M DAU, ~40% concurrently connected at peak.** This forces a sharded **persistent-connection (WebSocket) gateway** fleet, not a stateless HTTP service. *If this were ~10K users, I'd skip almost everything below and run one box with long-polling.*
2. **Both 1:1 and group chat, groups up to ~100K members (broadcast-list class).** This makes **fan-out the binding bottleneck** and forces a celebrity/large-group story. *If 1:1 only, I'd drop fan-out workers entirely.*
3. **Delivery + read receipts and presence are in scope; messages persist as history.** This adds three async event streams on top of plain delivery and makes the **store-then-deliver mailbox** core. *If receipts were out, the delivery path halves.*
4. **End-to-end encryption is assumed at the transport/payload layer; the server stores ciphertext and never needs to read message bodies.** This simplifies the server (no content indexing) but means **search and server-side moderation are out**. *If the server had to read content, we'd need a whole indexing tier.*

> 🤝 **In/out-of-scope checkpoint:** "In scope: 1:1 + group send, ordering, delivery+read receipts, presence, history, offline push. Out of scope: voice/video calls, media transcoding, full-text search over message bodies (we store ciphertext), and content moderation. Add or cut before I commit?"

### Functional requirements (derived from the locks — "above the line")

Since we assumed a huge connected fleet with group fan-out and receipts, the core flows are:

1. **Send & deliver a 1:1 message.** Sender posts a message; it lands on every recipient device in order.
   - 🗣️ **Plain words:** "I text my friend, and it shows up on their phone — and on their laptop too — within a second."
   - ⚡ **Why it's hard:** the recipient is connected to *some other gateway box* we have to find, and they might be offline, so we have to store first and route second.
2. **Group send & fan-out.** One message to a group lands on all members' devices.
   - 🗣️ **Plain words:** "I post in a 200-person group and everyone gets it once, in the same order."
   - ⚡ **Why it's hard:** one write becomes N deliveries; a 100K-member group is 200K deliveries from a single tap.
3. **Presence (online/last-seen).** Show whether a contact is online or when they were last seen.
   - 🗣️ **Plain words:** "I can see the little 'online' dot, or 'last seen 10:42'."
   - ⚡ **Why it's hard:** presence changes constantly and is read by everyone who has you as a contact — a naive design fans out a presence event to thousands per blink.
4. **Delivery & read receipts.** Sender sees one tick (sent), two ticks (delivered), blue ticks (read).
   - 🗣️ **Plain words:** "I can tell whether my message reached them and whether they've read it."
   - ⚡ **Why it's hard:** receipts are themselves messages flowing the *other* way, multiplying event volume, and must be ordered against the original.
5. **Message history & offline push.** Messages persist; if the recipient is offline they get a push notification and the backlog on reconnect.
   - 🗣️ **Plain words:** "If my phone was off, I still get the messages — and a notification — when I come back."
   - ⚡ **Why it's hard:** we must durably queue per-recipient and reconcile what was already delivered when they reconnect (no dupes, no gaps).

**Below the line (out of scope):** voice/video calling, media upload/transcoding pipeline, stickers/reactions storage, status/stories, server-side message search, content moderation, message editing/unsend (mention as an extension in §11).

### Non-Functional requirements (PROVISIONAL targets, tagged to assumptions)

- **Delivery latency: p99 < ~500ms gateway-to-gateway for an online recipient** (ASSUMING same-region; relaxes ~150ms per cross-region hop).
  - 🗣️ **Plain words:** "99 out of 100 messages land in under half a second when both people are online."
  - 💥 **What breaks without it:** chat feels broken — people retype, double-send, and abandon the app.
- **Ordering: per-conversation in-order delivery; no gaps, at-least-once with client dedup** (depends on the group-chat lock).
  - 🗣️ **Plain words:** "Messages in a chat always show up in the order they were sent — never scrambled."
  - 💥 **What breaks without it:** "ok" arrives before the question; threads become unreadable.
- **Availability: ~99.99% on the delivery path (four nines).**
  - 🗣️ **Plain words:** "Down less than ~1 hour per year total."
  - 💥 **What breaks without it:** a gateway outage silently drops messages — the worst possible failure for a messenger.
- **Durability: a message accepted by the server is never lost (RPO≈0).**
  - 🗣️ **Plain words:** "Once we say 'sent', that message survives a crash."
  - 💥 **What breaks without it:** users lose conversations — instant trust collapse.
- **Cost: drive cost-per-message toward fractions of a cent; the fan-out fleet, not storage, is the cost center.**
  - 🗣️ **Plain words:** "Each message should cost a tiny fraction of a cent to deliver."
  - 💥 **What breaks without it:** at ~20B messages/day the bill is unbounded and the product is unprofitable.

> ⚠️ **Gaps I'd flag out loud:** with E2E encryption assumed, I'm giving up server-side search and moderation — I'll note that in the ledger. And exactly-once delivery is impossible end-to-end; I'll do at-least-once + idempotent client dedup and call that out.

> 🎙️ **Script:** "So we're building WhatsApp-scale chat — 1:1 and groups up to a hundred thousand, with presence, receipts, history, and offline push. The customer expectation is dead simple: my message lands on every device, in order, in under a second, even if you're offline. The two things I care about most are durability — once we ack a message it's never lost — and the fan-out cost, because at twenty billion messages a day the delivery fleet is the whole budget. I'm assuming end-to-end encryption, so the server just shuttles ciphertext and never reads bodies, which keeps it simpler."

---

## 2. The Forks Behind My Assumptions (what changes if I'm wrong)

The three locks most likely to flip the design — I'm making them explicit so you can redirect.

**Fork 1 — Group size ceiling (I locked groups up to ~100K).**
- ↔️ **What changes if I'm wrong:**
  - If groups are really capped at ~256 (classic WhatsApp) → I can **fan-out-on-write** (push to each member's mailbox) cheaply and skip the celebrity machinery.
  - If we genuinely support 100K+ broadcast lists → fan-out-on-write would melt a single write into 200K deliveries, so I **must** add a **fan-out-on-read / async fan-out worker tier** for large groups. This is the whole §8 celebrity dive.
- 🗣️ **How I'd say it:** "If your biggest group is a few hundred people, I'll keep this simple and push to everyone on write — tell me if you actually have 100K broadcast lists, because that flips the fan-out design."

**Fork 2 — Connection model: do clients hold a persistent socket, or poll? (I locked persistent WebSocket.)**
- ↔️ **What changes if I'm wrong:**
  - If persistent connections are acceptable (mobile + web) → I keep a **stateful gateway** holding ~1M sockets each and a routing layer that knows which gateway holds which user.
  - If clients must poll (locked-down networks, no long-lived sockets) → I'd fall back to **long-polling against a per-user mailbox**, accept higher latency and far higher request volume, and the "which server holds the user" problem disappears (but cost/latency get worse).
- 🗣️ **How I'd say it:** "I'm assuming we can hold a persistent socket per device — that's what makes sub-second push possible. If your clients can't, say so and I'll switch to a polling mailbox and we'll eat the latency."

**Fork 3 — Single-region vs global (I locked single-region targets, global-aware).**
- ↔️ **What changes if I'm wrong:**
  - If single-region is fine → p99 budgets are tight and the routing table is one logical cluster.
  - If global → I need **region-pinned users**, cross-region message relay, and presence/receipts that tolerate ~150ms+ inter-region lag; ordering becomes per-conversation-per-region.
- 🗣️ **How I'd say it:** "I'll design region-aware but quote single-region latencies; if most chats cross continents, tell me and I'll add the cross-region relay and loosen the p99."

> 🤝 **Checkpoint:** "Those are the three places my design is sensitive — group ceiling, connection model, and geography. Want to pin any of them differently before I do the capacity math?"

---

## 3. Scale & Capacity (talkable numbers)

I deferred the math until now; I computed these in a small script and I'll only say the one or two numbers that drive a decision.

> **Glossary first:** **QPS** = queries (here, events) per second. **Concurrent connections** = how many devices hold an open socket *right now*. **Fan-out** = how many delivery events one sent message produces. **Burst factor** = peak ÷ average; I'll use 3x.

| Metric | Derivation (step-by-step) | Rounded | Talkable phrase | Decision it drives |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Peak concurrent sockets** | 500M DAU × 40% connected at peak = 200M | **~200M** | "two hundred million open sockets" | Gateway fleet: ~200M ÷ 1M sockets/box = ~200 boxes, +30% HA ≈ **~300 gateway nodes** |
| **Avg send QPS** | 500M × 40 msgs/day = 20B/day; 20B ÷ 86,400 ≈ 231K | **~230K/s** | "about a quarter-million sends a second" | Sizing the ingest/sequencer tier |
| **Peak send QPS** | 230K × 3 burst = 694K | **~700K/s** | "~700K sends a second at peak" | Ingest must shard; no single DB takes this |
| **Avg delivery (fan-out) QPS** | 230K sends × ~31 avg fan-out (70% 1:1×1 + 30% group×50, ×2 devices) ≈ 7.3M | **~7M/s** | "seven million deliveries a second" | **THE firehose — this is what the system actually moves** |
| **Peak delivery QPS** | 7.3M × 3 ≈ 21.8M | **~22M/s** | "twenty-plus million deliveries a second at peak" | Routing/fan-out tier MUST be sharded |
| **Big-group fan-out (one write)** | 100K members × 2 devices = 200K; a single 50K-events/s worker = ~4s to drain | **200K from one tap** | "one message becomes two hundred thousand deliveries" | Forces async fan-out workers + per-recipient mailbox |
| **Offline push QPS** | 7.3M deliveries × 55% offline ≈ 4M | **~4M/s** (peak ~12M/s) | "four million pushes a second" | Push relay must batch into APNs/FCM, not 1:1 |
| **Message history storage** | 20B/day × 400B × 3 repl × 1.4 idx × 1.5 compaction ≈ 50TB/day → 90-day ≈ 4.5PB | **~50 TB/day, ~4.5 PB at 90d (~$100K/mo)** | "about fifty terabytes a day" | Retention + partition-drop lifecycle (not the cost center) |
| **Unit economics** | ~$470K/mo infra ÷ (18.8T delivered/mo ÷ 1M) | **~$0.02 / 1M delivered** | "two cents per million messages" | Confirms fan-out fleet, not storage, is the budget |

**The ONE decision-forcing number:** **~22M delivery events/s at peak.** A single in-memory broker / routing shard tops out around **~150K ops/s**, so we need **~150 shards minimum** on the routing + fan-out tier.
**Flip threshold:** below ~150K/s total, a single broker and one route table would do — above it we **must** shard the routing/fan-out tier by user (and by conversation for groups). We're 100x past the flip, so sharding is not optional.

> 🎙️ **Script:** "The number that matters isn't sends — it's deliveries. We send about a quarter-million messages a second, but with group fan-out and two devices per person that's roughly seven million deliveries a second, twenty-plus million at peak. A single broker does maybe a hundred fifty thousand a second, so we're a hundred times past the point where one box works — the routing and fan-out tier has to be sharded by user from day one. Storage is almost a footnote here: fifty terabytes a day, about a hundred grand a month — the fan-out fleet is the real budget."

---

## 4. Core Entities

A quick scaffold — names and jobs only; full fields land in §7.

- **User / Device** — a person and their one-or-more logged-in devices; owns identity and which gateway/region each device is pinned to.
  - 🗣️ **Analogy:** "A person with a phone and a laptop — both need their own copy of every message."
  - ⚠️ **Probe:** "How do you deliver to multiple devices?" → each device is a separate delivery target with its own cursor/ack position.
- **Connection / Session** — a live socket: which gateway node currently holds this device's WebSocket.
  - 🗣️ **Analogy:** "A phone line that's currently plugged into switchboard #47 — to call this person we route to switchboard 47."
  - ⚠️ **Probe:** "How do you find which server holds a user?" → a routing registry (KV) maps userId→gatewayId, refreshed on connect/disconnect with a heartbeat TTL.
- **Conversation** — a 1:1 or group thread; owns membership and the ordering domain (the per-conversation sequence).
  - 🗣️ **Analogy:** "A WhatsApp chat — it has members and a strict message order inside it."
  - ⚠️ **Probe:** "How do you guarantee order?" → a per-conversation monotonic sequence number assigned at ingest.
- **Message** — one immutable chat message: id, conversationId, senderId, seq, ciphertext, timestamp.
  - 🗣️ **Analogy:** "A sealed envelope — the server moves and stores it but never opens it (it's encrypted)."
  - ⚠️ **Probe:** "How do you dedup retries?" → client-supplied messageId is the idempotency key.
- **Mailbox (per-device inbox)** — the durable per-device queue of message refs not yet acked by that device.
  - 🗣️ **Analogy:** "A pigeonhole at the post office — messages wait there until your device picks them up and confirms."
  - ⚠️ **Probe:** "What if the device was offline for a week?" → mailbox holds the backlog; on reconnect we replay from the device's last ack cursor.
- **Receipt / Presence event** — small status events (delivered, read, online, last-seen) that flow back and to contacts.
  - 🗣️ **Analogy:** "The little ticks and the 'online' dot — tiny status pings, not real messages."
  - ⚠️ **Probe:** "Don't receipts double your traffic?" → yes; we batch and coalesce them, and presence is pull-on-demand + heartbeat, not broadcast-on-every-blink.

> 🎙️ **Script:** "The entities are users-and-devices, the live connection that says which gateway holds you, the conversation that owns ordering, the immutable message itself, and the per-device mailbox that durably queues anything you haven't acked yet. The one people underestimate is the mailbox — it's what makes offline-then-reconnect work without dupes or gaps. I'll add full field-level schemas in the data model section."

---

## 5. API / Interface

Mostly a **persistent WebSocket** for the hot path (send/receive/receipts/presence) plus a few REST endpoints for setup and history. Default `/v1` prefix; cursor pagination on lists.

### 5.1 Open the connection — `GET /v1/connect` (Upgrade: websocket)
- **Why this verb:** an HTTP GET that upgrades to a WebSocket — a single long-lived, bidirectional pipe so the server can *push* without the client polling.
- **Key fields:** `Authorization: Bearer <JWT>` (caller identity — never a client-supplied userId), `deviceId` (which device), `lastAckSeq` per conversation (so the server knows what to replay).
- **One key decision:** on connect we **register userId→gatewayId in the routing KV** and **replay the mailbox backlog** from `lastAckSeq`. The connection IS the subscription.

```
// Client opens socket, then sends a hello frame
{ "type": "HELLO", "deviceId": "dev_a1", "cursors": { "conv_42": 1180 } }

// Server replays anything newer, then streams live
{ "type": "BACKLOG", "conversationId": "conv_42", "from": 1181, "to": 1190 }
```

### 5.2 Send a message — WS frame `SEND` (REST fallback: `POST /v1/messages`)
- **Why this verb:** POST creates new state (a new message). The client supplies a `messageId` (a UUID) so retries don't duplicate.
- **Key fields:** `conversationId` (target thread), `messageId` (client UUID — **idempotency key**), `ciphertext` (opaque blob; server never decrypts), `sentAt` (client clock, advisory only). **Absent on purpose:** the `seq` and authoritative `serverTs` — the **server assigns** these so clients can't forge ordering.
- **Concrete example:**
```
// Request (WS frame or POST /v1/messages)
{ "type": "SEND", "conversationId": "conv_42", "messageId": "5e2b...c9",
  "ciphertext": "base64blob==", "sentAt": "2026-06-21T10:42:01Z" }

// Response (server ACK back over the socket)
{ "type": "ACK", "messageId": "5e2b...c9", "seq": 1191,
  "serverTs": "2026-06-21T10:42:01.118Z", "status": "PERSISTED" }
```
- **One key decision:** **idempotency** — `messageId` is the natural key; a retried SEND returns the *same* `seq` and never creates a second row or a second fan-out.

### 5.3 Read receipt — WS frame `READ` (or `POST /v1/conversations/{id}/receipts`)
- **Why this verb:** POST appends a receipt event; it's idempotent on `(deviceId, conversationId, upToSeq)`.
- **Key fields:** `conversationId`, `upToSeq` (read watermark — "I've read everything through 1191"), `state` (`DELIVERED`|`READ`). Sending a *watermark* not per-message receipts collapses N receipts into one.
```
// Request
{ "type": "READ", "conversationId": "conv_42", "upToSeq": 1191 }
// Response
{ "type": "RECEIPT_ACK", "conversationId": "conv_42", "upToSeq": 1191 }
```
- **One key decision:** watermark semantics — one receipt event covers a range, cutting receipt traffic ~10x vs per-message ticks.

### 5.4 Presence — WS frames `PRESENCE_SUB` / `PRESENCE_PUSH`
- **Why:** clients subscribe to the presence of the contacts currently on screen only; server pushes deltas. Pull-on-demand, not global broadcast.
- **Key fields:** `subscribe: [userIds]` (only visible contacts), server replies with `online`/`lastSeen`.

### 5.5 Fetch history — `GET /v1/conversations/{id}/messages?cursor=...&limit=50`
- **Why this verb:** GET only reads. Cursor pagination (keyset on `seq`), never offset.
- **Object-level authz:** the server checks the **JWT caller is a member of `{id}`** before returning anything — "can THIS user read THIS conversation?"
```
// GET /v1/conversations/conv_42/messages?cursor=seq_1140&limit=50
{ "messages": [ {"seq":1141,"senderId":"u9","ciphertext":"..."} , ... ],
  "nextCursor": "seq_1191" }
```

**Cross-cutting (stated once):**
- **Versioning:** `/v1` prefix. Additive change (new optional frame field) = no bump; breaking change (renamed field, new ordering semantics) = `/v2` + dual-run.
- **Pagination:** cursor/keyset (`seq`-based) everywhere — offset pagination skips/dupes rows as new messages arrive under it.
- **Security:** caller identity is **always from the JWT**, never a client-sent userId. Prices-don't-apply here, but the equivalent trap is **forging sender or seq** — both are server-set. Object-level authz on every conversation read/write (membership check).

> 🎙️ **Script:** "The hot path is one persistent WebSocket per device — the client opens it, says 'here's where I left off,' and we replay the backlog and then stream live. Sends carry a client-generated message ID so retries are idempotent — same ID, same sequence number, no duplicate. The server, not the client, assigns the sequence number and the authoritative timestamp, so nobody can forge ordering. Receipts are watermarks — 'I've read through message 1191' — which collapses a hundred ticks into one event. History is a plain paginated GET, gated by a membership check."

---

## 6. High-Level Design (built one functional requirement at a time)

Same palette in every diagram: blue = clients, green = our services, amber = async/queues, grey = datastores, purple = external.

### 6.1 "Send & deliver a 1:1 message"

The interesting part: the recipient is connected to *a different gateway box*, and we must persist before we route so nothing is lost.

| Component | What it is (plain English) | Why THIS choice | What we DIDN'T pick & why | Trade-off we accept |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **WS Gateway** | A stateful server that holds millions of open device sockets and pushes frames down them | Sub-second push needs a held connection, not polling | Not HTTP long-poll (3-4x request overhead, worse latency) | Stateful — losing a box drops 1M sockets; needs reconnect logic |
| **Routing registry** | A KV store mapping userId/deviceId → gatewayId | We must find which box holds the recipient in <1ms | Not a DB query per message (too slow at 7M/s); not broadcast-to-all-gateways (wasteful) | Slightly stale on reconnect; fixed by short TTL + heartbeat |
| **Ingest / Sequencer** | A service that assigns the per-conversation seq and persists the message | Ordering needs ONE authority per conversation | Not client-assigned seq (forgeable, races); not a global counter (bottleneck) | Per-conversation serialization point (sharded by conversation) |
| **Message store** | Durable store for message bodies + per-device mailbox refs | RPO≈0 durability before we ack | Not in-memory only (loses data on crash) | Write amplification (mailbox fan-out refs) |

> **🗣️ Key terms for this slice:**
> **WebSocket** — a long-lived two-way pipe between client and server over one TCP connection. Here it lets us push a message the instant it arrives instead of the client asking "anything new?"
> **Routing registry** — a fast lookup table of "which gateway box currently holds this user." Here it turns "deliver to Bob" into "send to gateway #47."
> **Sequencer** — the one place that stamps each message in a conversation with the next number. Here it's what makes order deterministic.

**Diagram — 1:1 send and deliver**
```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Sender device]:::client
    B[Recipient device]:::client
    A -->|"1 SEND frame"| GW1[WS Gateway A]:::svc
    GW1 -->|"2 persist and seq"| ING[Ingest Sequencer]:::svc
    ING -->|"3 write message"| MS[(Message store)]:::store
    ING -->|"4 ack to sender"| GW1
    ING -->|"5 lookup recipient gateway"| RR[(Routing registry)]:::store
    ING -->|"6 enqueue delivery"| Q[[Delivery bus]]:::async
    Q -->|"7 route to gateway B"| GW2[WS Gateway B]:::svc
    GW2 -->|"8 push frame"| B
    GW2 -.->|"9 if offline write mailbox"| MB[(Per device mailbox)]:::store
    classDef client fill:#1a3a5c,stroke:#4A90D9,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    classDef svc fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    classDef async fill:#3d2a0b,stroke:#B7791F,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    classDef store fill:#2a2f3a,stroke:#6B7280,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
```
↳ new components: WS Gateway, Ingest/Sequencer, Routing registry, Delivery bus, Message store, Mailbox.

**Numbered narration:**
1. **Sender sends a SEND frame** over its existing socket. Gateway A owns this device's connection, so the frame lands there. *If the socket dropped mid-send, the client retries with the same messageId — idempotent.*
2. **Gateway A forwards to Ingest**, which assigns the next `seq` for `conv_42`. Ingest owns ordering for this conversation. State change: a seq is now reserved. *If Ingest is down, the gateway buffers briefly and the client sees PENDING — never a silent drop.*
3. **Ingest persists the message** to the durable store before acking. State change: the message is now durable (RPO≈0). *If the write fails, no ack is sent and the client retries.*
4. **Ingest acks the sender** with the assigned seq. The sender's one tick turns solid. *Duplicate SEND returns the same ack.*
5. **Ingest looks up the recipient's gateway** in the routing registry. *If the lookup misses (just reconnected elsewhere), we fall through to the mailbox.*
6. **Ingest enqueues a delivery event** on the bus. The bus decouples ingest speed from delivery speed (back-pressure lives here). *If the bus is saturated, we shed receipts/presence first, never message deliveries.*
7. **Delivery bus routes to Gateway B**, which holds the recipient. *If B is gone, the event re-routes after the registry TTL expires.*
8. **Gateway B pushes the frame** down the recipient's socket. State change: two ticks (delivered). 
9. **If the recipient is offline**, the delivery event writes to the per-device mailbox instead, to be replayed on reconnect (and triggers offline push — §6.5).

> 💬 **Say-while-drawing:** "The trick is *persist then route* — we durably store the message and ack the sender before we even know where the recipient is, so a connection blip can never lose a message."

> 🎙️ **Script:** "A send comes in on the sender's existing socket to its gateway. That gateway hands it to ingest, which stamps the next sequence number for that conversation and writes it durably — only then do we ack the sender. Now we look up which gateway holds the recipient and drop a delivery event on a bus; the recipient's gateway picks it up and pushes it down their socket. If they're offline, it goes into their mailbox and triggers a push instead. The one decision that makes this work is persisting before routing, so a dropped connection never loses a message — the cost is we always do one durable write before delivery."

### 6.2 "Group send & fan-out"

One write becomes N deliveries. For small groups, fan-out inline; for large groups, an async worker tier drains it so one tap doesn't block.

| Component | What it is | Why THIS | What we DIDN'T pick | Trade-off |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Fan-out workers** | A pool that expands one group message into per-member delivery events | A 100K-member group = 200K deliveries; can't do that inline | Not synchronous fan-out in ingest (blocks the send for seconds) | Async — recipients in a huge group see a slight delivery spread |
| **Group membership cache** | Redis copy of "who's in conv_42" | Fan-out needs the member list at 7M deliveries/s | Not a DB read per message (too slow) | Stale on membership change; refreshed on member events |

> **🗣️ Key terms for this slice:**
> **Fan-out worker** — a background process that takes "one message to group G" and turns it into one delivery per member. Here it keeps the sender's send fast while the heavy expansion happens behind the scenes.
> **Fan-out-on-write vs on-read** — write = push to every member's mailbox immediately; read = members pull when they connect. Here we use write for small groups, a hybrid for huge ones (§8).

**Diagram — group fan-out**
```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Sender device]:::client
    A -->|"1 SEND to group"| GW1[WS Gateway A]:::svc
    GW1 -->|"2 persist and seq"| ING[Ingest Sequencer]:::svc
    ING -->|"3 enqueue group event"| Q[[Delivery bus]]:::async
    Q -->|"4 pick up"| FW[Fan-out workers]:::svc
    FW -->|"5 read members"| GM[(Group member cache)]:::store
    FW -->|"6 per member delivery"| Q2[[Per recipient bus]]:::async
    Q2 -->|"7 route online"| GW2[WS Gateway B]:::svc
    Q2 -.->|"8 offline to mailbox"| MB[(Per device mailbox)]:::store
    classDef client fill:#1a3a5c,stroke:#4A90D9,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    classDef svc fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    classDef async fill:#3d2a0b,stroke:#B7791F,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    classDef store fill:#2a2f3a,stroke:#6B7280,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
```
↳ reuses existing: WS Gateway, Ingest, Delivery bus, Mailbox (from §6.1).

**Numbered narration:**
1-2. **Same as 1:1** through persist-and-seq — the message is stored once with one seq.
3. **Ingest enqueues a single group event** (not N events). State: one durable message, one bus event. *Keeps ingest O(1) per send regardless of group size.*
4. **Fan-out workers pick it up.** They own the expansion. *If a worker dies mid-fan-out, the event is re-delivered; per-recipient writes are idempotent on (messageId, deviceId).*
5. **Workers read the member list** from the cache. *Cache miss → fall back to the membership DB, slower but correct.*
6-8. **Workers emit one delivery per member device** onto the per-recipient bus; online → gateway push, offline → mailbox. *A 100K group is drained across many workers in parallel.*

> 💬 **Say-while-drawing:** "Ingest stays O(1) per send — it writes one message and one event. The N-way explosion happens in the worker pool, off the send path, so a giant group never makes my send slow."

> 🎙️ **Script:** "For groups, the key move is keeping the send itself O(1): ingest writes the message once and drops a single group event. The fan-out workers do the expensive part — read the member list and emit one delivery per device — but off the critical path. Small groups drain in milliseconds; a hundred-thousand-member broadcast spreads across many workers over a second or two, which is fine. The trade-off is delivery isn't perfectly simultaneous for huge groups, but the alternative — fanning out inline — would freeze the sender for seconds."

### 6.3 "Presence (online / last-seen)"

Presence is high-churn and read-heavy. The trap is broadcasting every state change to everyone.

| Component | What it is | Why THIS | What we DIDN'T pick | Trade-off |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Presence store** | Redis with userId → {state, lastSeen, ttl} updated by gateway heartbeats | Reads must be sub-ms; TTL auto-expires stale "online" | Not a DB (too slow, too chatty) | Eventually consistent — "online" can lag a few seconds |
| **Presence subscription** | Per-connection set of contacts the client is currently viewing | Only push presence for visible contacts, not all contacts | Not broadcast-on-change to all contacts (fan-out storm) | Client must subscribe/unsubscribe as it scrolls |

> **🗣️ Key terms for this slice:**
> **Heartbeat** — a tiny periodic ping from the gateway that refreshes a TTL. Here, if it stops, the TTL expires and the user flips to offline automatically — no explicit "I'm leaving" needed.
> **TTL (time-to-live)** — how long a value lives before auto-expiring. Here a ~30s TTL on the presence key means a crashed device shows offline within 30s.

**Diagram — presence**
```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Viewer device]:::client
    B[Contact device]:::client
    B -->|"1 heartbeat"| GW2[WS Gateway B]:::svc
    GW2 -->|"2 set state ttl 30s"| PS[(Presence store)]:::store
    A -->|"3 subscribe visible contacts"| GW1[WS Gateway A]:::svc
    GW1 -->|"4 read presence"| PS
    GW1 -->|"5 push presence delta"| A
    PS -.->|"6 ttl expiry flips offline"| GW1
    classDef client fill:#1a3a5c,stroke:#4A90D9,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    classDef svc fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    classDef store fill:#2a2f3a,stroke:#6B7280,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
```
↳ reuses existing: WS Gateway (from §6.1).

**Numbered narration:**
1-2. **Contact's gateway heartbeats** every ~15s, refreshing a ~30s TTL key. State: presence is "fresh." *If the device crashes, no heartbeat → TTL expires → offline.*
3-4. **Viewer subscribes only to on-screen contacts**; gateway reads their presence. *Scrolling away unsubscribes — bounds the fan-out.*
5. **Gateway pushes presence deltas** to the viewer. 
6. **TTL expiry** flips the contact offline without any explicit event.

> 💬 **Say-while-drawing:** "Presence is just a TTL key refreshed by heartbeats — online means 'heartbeat seen recently,' offline means 'TTL expired.' And I only push presence for contacts you're actually looking at, so a celebrity going online doesn't blast a million people."

> 🎙️ **Script:** "Presence is deliberately cheap. The gateway heartbeats a short-TTL key, so 'online' just means we've seen a heartbeat recently and 'offline' is the TTL expiring — no explicit logout needed. The thing that keeps it from melting down is subscribing only to the contacts you're currently viewing, not all of them, so someone with a million followers coming online doesn't trigger a million pushes."

### 6.4 "Delivery & read receipts"

Receipts are messages flowing backward. Watermarks keep the volume sane.

> ↳ builds on §6.1 diagram — receipts reuse the exact send/deliver path in reverse. A `READ upToSeq=1191` frame goes recipient → gateway → ingest → routing → sender's gateway → sender. New state: the conversation's per-device read watermark advances; the sender's ticks turn blue. The only new component is a small **receipt aggregator** that coalesces a burst of receipts into one watermark update before fanning back. *What could go wrong:* receipts racing the original message — we order them by the same per-conversation seq, so a READ for seq 1191 is ignored until 1191 exists.

> 💬 **Say-while-drawing:** "Receipts run the same rails backward, but as a watermark — 'read through 1191' — so I never send a hundred separate blue-tick events."

> 🎙️ **Script:** "Receipts reuse the delivery path in reverse — they're just tiny status frames. The one optimization is the watermark: instead of a receipt per message I send 'I've read through 1191,' which collapses a burst into one event and roughly halves what would otherwise double our traffic. They're ordered by the same sequence numbers, so a read receipt can never arrive before the message it's acking."

### 6.5 "Message history & offline push"

Persist everything; if offline, push a notification and replay on reconnect.

| Component | What it is | Why THIS | What we DIDN'T pick | Trade-off |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Push relay** | Service that batches offline deliveries into APNs/FCM calls | Offline users (~55%) need a notification, not a socket | Not 1:1 calls to APNs/FCM (rate limits, cost) | Push is best-effort; the durable mailbox is the source of truth |
| **APNs / FCM** | Apple/Google push gateways (external) | Only path to a backgrounded phone | Not SMS (cost, no rich payload) | Third-party — can throttle/fail; we degrade to "fetch on next open" |

> **🗣️ Key terms for this slice:**
> **APNs / FCM** — Apple Push Notification service and Firebase Cloud Messaging: the OS-level pipes that wake a backgrounded app. Here they nudge an offline phone so the user knows to open the app and pull the backlog.
> **Mailbox cursor** — a per-device pointer to "last seq this device acked." Here it's how we replay exactly the missed messages on reconnect with no dupes and no gaps.

**Diagram — offline push and reconnect replay**

```mermaid
    MB -->|"2 trigger push"| PR[Push relay]:::svc

    EXT -->|"4 wake app"| D
    D -->|"5 reconnect with cursor"| GW[WS Gateway]:::svc
    GW -->|"6 read backlog from cursor"| MB
    GW -->|"7 replay missed"| D
    D -->|"8 ack advances cursor"| MB

    classDef async fill:#3d2a0b,stroke:#B7791F,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    classDef store fill:#2a2f3a,stroke:#6B7280,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    classDef ext fill:#3a2a4a,stroke:#9B6BC1,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
```
↳ reuses existing: Per-recipient bus, Mailbox, WS Gateway (from §6.1–6.2).

**Numbered narration:**
1. **Offline recipient** → delivery lands in the mailbox. *Durable, so nothing is lost.*
2-3. **Mailbox triggers the push relay**, which **batches** notifications to APNs/FCM. *If APNs throttles, we drop the notify but keep the mailbox entry — user still gets it on next open.*
4-5. **OS wakes the app; device reconnects** sending its last cursor. 
6-7. **Gateway replays everything after the cursor** from the mailbox. *Idempotent on messageId, so a partial earlier delivery won't double up.*
8. **Device acks; cursor advances** and acked entries become eligible for cleanup.

> 💬 **Say-while-drawing:** "The mailbox plus a per-device cursor is the whole offline story — push is just a nudge; the cursor is what guarantees we replay exactly the missed messages, no dupes, no gaps."

> 🎙️ **Script:** "When a recipient's offline, the delivery still lands durably in their mailbox and we batch a push through APNs or FCM to wake the app. The push itself is best-effort — if Apple throttles us, no big deal, because the mailbox is the source of truth. On reconnect the device says 'I'm at cursor 1180,' and we replay everything after that. Because every message carries a client-side ID, replaying something that was half-delivered before just dedupes — no double messages."

## 7. Data Model & Storage

**PART A — Entity table**

| Entity | Key fields (PK / shard key annotated) | Chosen store | Shard / partition key | Consistency |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Message | **messageId (PK, client UUID)**, conversationId, senderId, **seq**, ciphertext, serverTs | Cassandra (wide-column) | **conversationId** | Strong within conversation (seq), eventual across |
| Conversation | **conversationId (PK)**, type, memberIds, createdAt | Cassandra + Redis cache | conversationId | Strong on membership writes |
| Mailbox entry | **(deviceId, seq) (PK)**, messageId, state | Cassandra | **deviceId** | At-least-once; idempotent on messageId |
| Routing | **userId/deviceId (PK)** → gatewayId, ttl | Redis (in-memory KV) | userId | Eventual (TTL-bounded) |
| Presence | **userId (PK)** → state, lastSeen, ttl | Redis | userId | Eventual (~seconds) |
| Read watermark | **(conversationId, deviceId) (PK)** → upToSeq | Cassandra + Redis | conversationId | Last-writer-wins (monotonic) |

**PART B — Storage decision cards**

**🗄️ Cassandra — used for: Message, Conversation, Mailbox, Read watermark**
1. **What it is:** a distributed wide-column store optimized for very high write throughput and time-ordered data, partitioned across many nodes with tunable replication.
2. **Why we picked it — the access pattern:** our dominant access is "write a message, then read a conversation's messages ordered by seq" at ~700K writes/s peak. Cassandra's partition-by-conversationId + clustering-by-seq gives O(1) append and a clean ordered range read, and it scales writes horizontally with no single primary.
3. **Considered instead:**
   - **PostgreSQL** — great for the sequencer's small ordered counters, but a single primary can't take 700K writes/s and 50TB/day without painful manual sharding.
   - **DynamoDB** — would work, but cross-region replication control and cost at this volume favor self-run Cassandra; also avoids deep vendor lock-in.
   - **Kafka as the store** — it's the bus, not the queryable history; you can't paginate a user's chat from a topic.
4. **Trade-off:** no cross-partition joins and only tunable (not free strong) consistency; we denormalize the mailbox and accept LWW on watermarks.
5. **🗣️ How to say it:** "Messages go in Cassandra, partitioned by conversation and clustered by sequence number — that gives me cheap appends and ordered reads at write rates a single SQL primary could never take. I looked at Postgres but it can't absorb 700K writes a second without sharding pain, and Dynamo's fine but I'd rather not lock in at this volume."

**🗄️ Redis — used for: Routing registry, Presence, hot caches**
1. **What it is:** an in-memory key-value store with sub-millisecond reads and native TTLs.
2. **Why we picked it:** routing and presence are read on *every* delivery (7M/s) and must be sub-ms; TTLs give us automatic staleness expiry (heartbeat-or-gone) for free.
3. **Considered instead:** **A SQL/Cassandra lookup per message** (way too slow at 7M/s); **ZooKeeper/etcd** for routing (built for strong coordination, not 7M reads/s of ephemeral state).
4. **Trade-off:** in-memory means data is lost on node failure — acceptable because routing/presence are reconstructed from reconnects and heartbeats within seconds.
5. **🗣️ How to say it:** "Routing — which gateway holds you — and presence both live in Redis because they're read on every single delivery and have to be sub-millisecond, and the TTL gives me 'offline' for free when heartbeats stop. If a Redis node dies, no drama: it rebuilds from reconnects in seconds."

**🗄️ Kafka (or equivalent log) — used for: Delivery bus, Per-recipient bus**
1. **What it is:** a durable, partitioned append-only log that buffers events so a slow consumer never loses data.
2. **Why we picked it:** the delivery firehose (7M–22M/s) needs a buffer that decouples ingest from delivery and survives consumer slowdowns; partitioning by recipient gives ordered, parallel drain.
3. **Considered instead:** **RabbitMQ** (lower throughput ceiling, weaker partitioned ordering at this scale); **direct gateway-to-gateway RPC** (no buffer — a slow recipient gateway back-pressures the sender).
4. **Trade-off:** at-least-once delivery (consumers can re-read), so consumers must be idempotent — fine because messageId dedups.
5. **🗣️ How to say it:** "Between ingest and delivery I put a partitioned log — Kafka-style — so a backed-up recipient gateway never stalls senders, and I can drain in parallel. It's at-least-once, so consumers dedupe on message ID."

**PART C — Per-operation consistency**

| Operation | Store | Consistency level | Why right here |
|---|---|---|---|
| Assign message seq | Sequencer + Cassandra | **Strong (per conversation)** | Ordering is correctness-critical |
| Persist message body | Cassandra | **Quorum write (RPO≈0)** | Money-equivalent: never lose an acked message |
| Read conversation history | Cassandra | Quorum read | Must reflect what was acked |
| Routing lookup | Redis | Eventual (TTL) | A stale entry falls through to mailbox — self-correcting |
| Presence read | Redis | Eventual (~seconds) | A few seconds stale is fine for a dot |
| Read-your-own-writes | Gateway returns assigned seq inline | Strong | The sender sees its own message immediately via the ACK, before any replica lag matters |

**Read-your-own-writes:** the sender gets the assigned `seq` in the synchronous ACK (step 4 of §6.1), so its own message is visible immediately without waiting for replication. Other devices of the same user receive it via normal fan-out (their own mailbox entry).

**PART D — Data lifecycle (largest table = Message, ~50 TB/day)**
At ~50 TB/day, **90-day retention caps us at ~4.5 PB (~$100K/mo)**; without a bound we grow unbounded. Cassandra tables are **time-partitioned by month**, and we reclaim via **partition/SSTable drop with per-table TTL — O(1), not row-by-row DELETE** (row deletes create tombstones that churn compaction and never reclaim cleanly). Older-than-90-day ciphertext that must be retained for compliance is archived to **S3/Glacier** (cold, cheap), and E2E means erasure is **crypto-shredding** — drop the per-user/per-conversation key and the ciphertext is permanently unreadable everywhere (replicas, caches, archive) at once.

> 🎙️ **Script:** "The core store is Cassandra — partitioned by conversation, clustered by sequence — because it eats 700K writes a second and gives ordered reads, which a single Postgres primary can't. Routing and presence sit in Redis for sub-millisecond, TTL-driven lookups on every delivery. Between ingest and delivery is a partitioned log so a slow consumer never stalls the sender. I considered Dynamo for the messages but chose self-run Cassandra to control cost and cross-region at this volume. And the message table — the only big one — is time-partitioned so we reclaim space with an instant partition drop, not slow row deletes; erasure is crypto-shredding the key."

## 8. Deep Dives — Bad → Good → Great

> 🆘 **If you get stuck:** go back to the binding bottleneck — ~22M deliveries/s and "which gateway holds the recipient" — and reason forward from there.

### How do we find which server holds a user and deliver to it at 22M/s?

**#### Bad: broadcast to all gateways**
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** to deliver to Bob, shout the message at every gateway box and let the one holding Bob keep it.
- **Approach:** publish each delivery to all ~300 gateways; each checks its local socket table.
- **Why people try this:** no routing state to maintain.
- ⚠️ **What breaks:** 22M deliveries × 300 gateways = ~6.6B internal messages/s — the internal network melts. Pure waste.
- 🔁 **Forces upgrade:** the moment you have more than a handful of gateways.

**#### Good: routing registry in Redis**
- ↩️ **What the previous tier got wrong:** broadcasting touched every box; we only need to touch the *one* box holding the recipient.
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** keep a phonebook of "which switchboard each person is plugged into" and call just that one.
- **Approach:** on connect, the gateway writes `userId/deviceId → gatewayId` (TTL ~60s, refreshed by heartbeat) into Redis. Delivery looks up the gateway and routes one event to it via the bus.
- ⚠️ **What breaks:** a single Redis instance caps ~150K ops/s; at 7M lookups/s it's 50x over. And on a gateway crash, 1M stale entries point at a dead box until TTL expires — those messages bounce to the mailbox meanwhile.
- 🔁 **Forces upgrade:** the 7M/s lookup rate blows past one Redis node.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ING[Ingest]:::svc -->|"1 lookup user"| RR[(Routing registry)]:::store
    RR -->|"2 gatewayId"| ING
    ING -->|"3 route one event"| Q[[Delivery bus]]:::async
    Q -->|"4 to that gateway"| GW[WS Gateway]:::svc
    classDef svc fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    classDef async fill:#3d2a0b,stroke:#B7791F,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    classDef store fill:#2a2f3a,stroke:#6B7280,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
```

**#### Great: consistent-hash-sharded routing KV + LB-driven gateway assignment**
- ↩️ **What the previous tier got wrong:** one Redis node can't take 7M lookups/s, and a crash strands 1M users.
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** split the phonebook across ~150 machines by user so lookups scale, while the gateway each user lands on is whatever the load balancer picked — recorded in the phonebook on connect, not computed from a hash.
- **Approach:** two distinct concerns kept separate. **(a) Gateway assignment is load-balancer-driven, not hash-of-userId:** a client connects to whatever gateway the L4 load balancer / nearest-PoP routing hands it, and on connect the gateway *records* `userId/deviceId → gatewayId` in the routing registry. There is no formula that derives a user's gateway — that is exactly why we need the registry lookup at all. **(b) Shard the routing KV by `consistent-hash(userId)` across ~150+ nodes** (each ~150K ops/s → ~22M/s headroom); *consistent hashing here spreads the KV keyspace over a ring of nodes so adding or removing a routing node only moves ~1/N of the keys, not all of them.* On gateway death, its users reconnect — the load balancer places them on healthy gateways and they re-register the new mapping; the bus retries strand-bound events after TTL. Hot users (a celebrity's own device) are still one socket — fine; the hot problem is groups (next dive).
- 🔢 **Decision math:** 22M peak lookups/s ÷ 150K per node ≈ **150 shards**; +30% HA ≈ ~200 routing nodes. Gateway crash blast radius = 1M sockets = 0.5% of the fleet, recovered in <TTL (~60s) via reconnect.
- ✅ **Failure matrix:**

| Scenario | What happens |
|---|---|
| Recipient online | Lookup hits, one event routed, pushed in <500ms |
| Recipient just reconnected elsewhere | Old entry stale → event bounces to mailbox → replayed; no loss |
| Gateway crashes | 1M sockets drop; clients auto-reconnect; entries re-registered within TTL |
| Routing shard down | Affected users' deliveries fall through to mailbox (durable) until shard heals |
| Duplicate delivery (bus re-read) | Client dedups on messageId |

> 🎙️ **Script:** "Delivery is a phonebook problem. The naive version broadcasts to every gateway and melts the network. The fix is a routing registry — on connect, a gateway records 'I hold user X' in Redis, and delivery routes one event to exactly that box. Note the gateway a user lands on is whatever the load balancer picked, not a hash of their ID — if it were a hash I wouldn't need the registry at all, I'd just compute it. The registry exists precisely because placement is dynamic. But 7 million lookups a second is 50x what one Redis node does, so I shard the registry itself by consistent-hash of userId across ~150 nodes, which lets me add or remove a routing node and only move a small slice of keys. When a gateway dies, its users reconnect, the load balancer places them on healthy boxes, and they re-register within a minute. Anything that can't be routed falls through to the durable mailbox — so worst case is a slight delay, never a lost message."
> 🧠 **If they ask "what about the stale-entry window after a crash?":** "Those deliveries bounce to the mailbox and replay on reconnect — the routing table is an optimization, the mailbox is the source of truth."

### How do we keep messages in order within a conversation?

**#### Good: per-conversation sequence at ingest**
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** one stamping machine per conversation hands out 1, 2, 3 in order; clients sort by that number.
- **Approach:** ingest shards by `conversationId`; the owning shard assigns a monotonic `seq`. Clients render by `seq`, buffering a brief gap. At-least-once + messageId dedup means re-deliveries don't reorder.
- ⚠️ **What breaks:** if the per-conversation owner moves (rebalance), two ingest nodes could briefly both think they own it and assign overlapping seqs.
- 🔁 **Forces upgrade:** need a clean ownership/fencing story.

**#### Great: single-owner shard + fencing token**
- ↩️ **Previous got wrong:** ownership handoff could double-assign seqs.
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** only one machine owns a conversation at a time, and it carries a version stamp so a stale old owner's writes get rejected.
- **Approach:** conversation→shard ownership is leased; each lease has a **fencing token** *(a monotonically increasing number attached to the lease — the store rejects any write carrying an older token, so a stale owner can't corrupt order)*. Seq is a per-conversation counter co-located with the data (Cassandra lightweight transaction or a per-shard counter), so it's never a global coordinator.
- 🔢 **Decision math:** sharding by conversationId across ingest means no single global sequencer — each conversation's ~few msgs/s is trivial; the hottest single conversation (a 100K group) is still one writer at maybe tens of msgs/s. No global bottleneck.
- ✅ **Failure matrix:**

| Scenario | What happens |
|---|---|
| Normal | seq assigned in order, client renders in order |
| Gap on client | Client waits briefly / refetches missing seq via history GET |
| Ownership handoff | Old owner's writes fenced off by stale token; new owner continues seq |
| Duplicate send | Same messageId → same seq returned, no reorder |

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    GW[WS Gateway]:::svc -->|"1 send"| S[Conversation shard owner]:::svc
    S -->|"2 assign next seq"| C[(Seq counter co-located)]:::store
    S -->|"3 write with fencing token"| MS[(Message store)]:::store
    MS -.->|"4 reject stale token"| S
    classDef svc fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    classDef store fill:#2a2f3a,stroke:#6B7280,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
```

> 🎙️ **Script:** "Ordering is solved by having exactly one owner per conversation stamp a monotonic sequence number — clients just sort by it. The subtle bug is during a rebalance, when two nodes might briefly both think they own the conversation; I guard that with a fencing token, a version stamp on the lease, so the store rejects writes from a stale owner. Because I shard by conversation, there's no global sequencer to bottleneck — each conversation is its own tiny ordering domain."
> 🧠 **If they ask "why not global timestamps?":** "Clock skew across servers would reorder messages; a per-conversation counter is exact and cheap."

### How do we handle the celebrity / 100K-group fan-out?

**#### Bad: synchronous fan-out-on-write inline**
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** when someone posts to a huge group, push to all 100K members right there before acking.
- **Approach:** ingest loops over members, writing each delivery before returning.
- ⚠️ **What breaks:** 200K deliveries at 50K/s = ~4s; the sender's send blocks for 4 seconds, and ingest threads pile up — connection pool exhausts, the conversation shard stalls for everyone.
- 🔁 **Forces upgrade:** any large group makes sends unusably slow.

**#### Good: async fan-out workers**
- ↩️ **Previous got wrong:** the N-way explosion was on the critical send path.
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** ack the sender instantly, then a pool of workers does the heavy delivery in the background.
- **Approach:** ingest writes one message + one group event; workers expand it across many machines in parallel.
- ⚠️ **What breaks:** a single mega-group's event still goes to one worker partition → that one worker is slow; and re-reading the 100K member list per message is heavy.
- 🔁 **Forces upgrade:** the hot single group concentrates on one partition.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ING[Ingest]:::svc -->|"1 one group event"| Q[[Delivery bus]]:::async
    Q -->|"2 fan-out"| FW[Fan-out workers]:::svc
    FW -->|"3 read members"| GM[(Group member cache)]:::store
    FW -->|"4 per device deliveries"| Q2[[Per recipient bus]]:::async
    classDef svc fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    classDef async fill:#3d2a0b,stroke:#B7791F,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    classDef store fill:#2a2f3a,stroke:#6B7280,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
```

**#### Great: hybrid — sharded fan-out + pull-on-read for mega-groups**
- ↩️ **Previous got wrong:** one mega-group concentrated on one worker partition.
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** split a giant group's member list into chunks fanned out by many workers in parallel; and for truly huge broadcast lists, don't push at all — let members pull the latest when they open the chat.
- **Approach:**
  - **Chunked parallel fan-out:** a mega-group event is split into member-range chunks (e.g. 1K each); each chunk is an independent bus event drained by a different worker — 200K deliveries across 200 workers = ~1s.
  - **Pull-on-read threshold:** above a member count (say ~10K), switch that conversation to **fan-out-on-read** — store the message once, and each member's client pulls new messages on open (history GET) rather than receiving a push. This trades a tiny "open the app to see it" delay for eliminating the 200K-write storm.
  - **Hot-key relief:** the group member cache is replicated read-only so 200 workers reading the same member list don't hammer one Redis node.
- 🔢 **Decision math:** naive single worker = 200K ÷ 50K/s = **4s**; chunked across 200 workers = ~1s. Above ~10K members, fan-out-on-write cost (N writes/msg) exceeds fan-out-on-read cost (1 write + pulls), so we flip per-conversation at that threshold.
- ✅ **Failure matrix:**

| Scenario | What happens |
|---|---|
| 200-member group | Fan-out-on-write, all delivered in ms |
| 100K broadcast list | Fan-out-on-read: stored once, members pull on open |
| Worker dies mid-chunk | Chunk re-delivered; per-device writes idempotent on messageId |
| Member-list cache hot | Read-replicated; workers read from replicas |
| Mixed online/offline | Online pull-or-push; offline get push + mailbox |

```mermaid
    SP -->|"2 member range chunks"| Q[[Delivery bus]]:::async
    Q -->|"3 parallel drain"| FW[Fan-out workers]:::svc
    FW -->|"4 read replica members"| GMR[(Member cache replicas)]:::store
    FW -->|"5 small groups push"| Q2[[Per recipient bus]]:::async
    ING -.->|"6 mega groups store once"| MS[(Message store)]:::store
    MS -.->|"7 members pull on open"| Q2

    classDef store fill:#2a2f3a,stroke:#6B7280,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
```

> 🎙️ **Script:** "Big groups are the celebrity problem. Inline fan-out blocks the sender for seconds and exhausts the pool, so first I move the N-way explosion to async workers off the send path. But one mega-group still piles onto one worker, so I split its member list into chunks fanned out across hundreds of workers in parallel — a hundred-thousand-member blast drains in about a second. And past roughly ten thousand members I flip that conversation to fan-out-on-read: store the message once and let people pull it when they open the chat, which kills the write storm entirely. The trade-off is a tiny 'open to see it' delay for the biggest broadcasts, which is exactly where simultaneity matters least."
> 🧠 **If they ask "how do you pick the 10K threshold?":** "It's where the cost of N pushes exceeds 1 store + lazy pulls — I'd tune it from real fan-out latency and write-amplification metrics."

### How do we prevent duplicates and guarantee delivery (idempotency)?

**#### Great: client messageId + idempotent consumers + cursors**
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** every message carries a unique ID from the sender; the server and every device ignore an ID they've already seen.
- **Approach:** the client generates `messageId` (UUID). Ingest upserts on it (dup SEND → same seq, no second fan-out). Delivery is at-least-once over the bus; the **per-device mailbox is keyed on (deviceId, messageId)** so a re-delivery is a no-op. The reconnect **cursor** guarantees no gaps. End-to-end this is **at-least-once + idempotent dedup**, which is the achievable equivalent of exactly-once.
- 🔢 **Decision math:** zero shared-namespace collisions — UUIDs are globally unique; auto-generated server seq is per-conversation and never shown to clients as an idempotency key.
- ✅ **Failure matrix:**

| Scenario | What happens |
|---|---|
| Sender retries (network blip) | Same messageId → server returns original seq, no dup |
| Bus re-delivers | Mailbox dedups on (deviceId, messageId) |
| Worker crashes after partial fan-out | Re-run; already-written entries are no-ops |
| Device replays after reconnect | Cursor + messageId → exact missed set, no dupes/gaps |

> 🎙️ **Script:** "Exactly-once is a myth end-to-end, so I do the achievable thing: at-least-once plus idempotent dedup. The client mints a message ID; the server upserts on it so a retry returns the original sequence number with no second fan-out, and every device's mailbox dedups on that same ID. Combined with the reconnect cursor, you get no duplicates and no gaps — which is exactly-once in practice."
> 🧠 **If they ask "what stops two devices of the sender from colliding IDs?":** "UUIDs are globally unique; even concurrent sends from two of my devices get distinct IDs."

### How do we shed load and apply back-pressure under overload?

**#### Great: bounded queues + tiered shedding**
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** when we're drowning, we drop the least important traffic first — receipts and presence — and always protect actual message delivery.
- **Approach:** the delivery bus is **bounded**; when a partition's lag/queue depth crosses a threshold, the gateway **sheds in order**: (1) presence updates, (2) read receipts, (3) typing indicators — never message deliveries. Senders see **429 + Retry-After** if ingest itself is over capacity. Trigger signal = consumer lag + p99 breach.
- 🔢 **Decision math:** presence + receipts are ~40% of event volume; shedding them buys ~40% headroom instantly before touching a single real message.
- ✅ **Failure matrix:** overload → presence stops updating (dots stale), then ticks lag, then if still over, ingest 429s senders with Retry-After; messages already accepted are never dropped.

> 🎙️ **Script:** "Under overload I shed by importance: presence first, then receipts, then typing — message delivery is the last thing I'd ever drop. Those status events are about forty percent of the volume, so shedding them buys big headroom before I touch correctness. If ingest itself is saturated I push back on senders with a 429 and a Retry-After, so the client backs off instead of hammering."
> 🧠 **If they ask "won't users notice presence freezing?":** "Yes, and that's the point — a stale 'online' dot is invisible compared to a dropped message."

## 9. Reliability, Failure Modes & Cost

**9A — Availability targets (what the nines mean)**
- **Delivery path: four nines (99.99%) = ~52 minutes/year of downtime total.** Mechanisms: ~300 gateways behind L4 load balancers across **multi-AZ** *(multiple data centres in one region — if one building loses power, another takes over automatically)*; clients auto-reconnect to any healthy gateway; the durable mailbox means an outage delays, not drops, messages.
- **Write/persist path: four-plus nines**, via quorum writes across 3 replicas so no single node loss loses an acked message.

**9B — Per-dependency failure table**

| Component | What breaks if down | Graceful degradation (what the USER SEES) | Recovery |
|---|---|---|---|
| WS Gateway | 1M sockets drop | App shows "connecting…", auto-reconnects to another box in seconds; no messages lost (mailbox) | Reconnect re-registers routing; bus replays |
| Routing Redis shard | Can't find some recipients' gateways | Their messages route to mailbox → delivered slightly late | Shard heals or fails over; reconnects rebuild entries |
| Ingest/sequencer shard | Sends for those conversations stall | Sender sees "sending…" (PENDING), not an error | Shard ownership re-leased; queued sends drain |
| Delivery bus | Deliveries pause | Messages persisted but not yet pushed — appear on next drain | Consumers resume from offset, at-least-once |
| Cassandra node | Reduced write capacity | Quorum still served by other replicas; no user-visible change | Replica repair / node replace |
| APNs/FCM | No offline notifications | User doesn't get a banner but sees messages when they open the app | Retry; mailbox is source of truth |

**9C — RPO / RTO (plain definitions first)**
> **RPO (Recovery Point Objective)** = how much data we'd lose if we crashed *right now*. RPO≈0 = we lose nothing because we replicate before acking.
> **RTO (Recovery Time Objective)** = how long until the service works again after a crash.

| Data class | RPO | RTO | Mechanism |
|---|---|---|---|
| Messages (acked) | **≈0** | <1 min | Quorum write to 3 replicas before ACK; gateway reconnect |
| Mailbox/cursors | ≈0 | <1 min | Same Cassandra quorum |
| Routing/presence (Redis) | seconds OK | seconds | Rebuilt from reconnects + heartbeats — intentionally not durable |
| In-flight bus events | ≈0 (logged) | seconds | Consumers resume from committed offset |

**9D — Cost breakdown**
Top 3 drivers:
1. **Fan-out + gateway fleet (~$365K/mo of ~$470K)** — ~300 gateways holding 200M sockets + ~1,500 fan-out workers draining 22M deliveries/s. This is the budget, by far.
2. **Storage (~$100K/mo)** — ~50 TB/day, 90-day retention ~4.5 PB. Big but secondary.
3. **Push egress** — APNs/FCM are free, but the relay fleet and bandwidth add up at 4M pushes/s.
**Unit economics: ~$0.02 per 1M messages delivered** (computed). **Biggest optimization:** the fan-out-on-read flip for mega-groups — it removes the largest write-amplification source, the single biggest lever on the fan-out fleet.

**Observability:** golden signals = **latency** (p99 send→deliver), **traffic** (deliveries/s), **errors** (failed pushes, fenced writes), **saturation** (bus lag, socket count/gateway). **Trace-id** is minted at the gateway and propagated through ingest → bus → worker → delivering gateway so one message is traceable across the async hop. **Burn-rate alert** on the four-nines SLO. **Rate-limiting:** token-bucket per user at ingest (state in Redis — itself a sharded coordinator), **429 + Retry-After** on breach.

> 🎙️ **Script:** "When a gateway dies the user just sees 'connecting' for a couple seconds and reconnects elsewhere — no message lost, because everything durable lives in the mailbox. If routing Redis hiccups, those messages route to the mailbox and arrive a beat late. If Apple's push service is down, you don't get a banner but everything's waiting when you open the app. The thing I never compromise is the acked-message durability — quorum-replicated before we ever say 'sent,' so RPO is basically zero."

## 10. Trade-off Ledger

**🔀 Decision: Hybrid fan-out (write for small, read for mega-groups) vs pure fan-out-on-write**
1. **Chose & why:** keeps small-group delivery instant while making 100K-member broadcasts survivable.
2. **Gave up:** a single uniform code path; mega-group messages have a slight pull delay.
3. **🗣️ Plain words:** "For normal chats I deliver instantly; for giant broadcasts I let people grab it when they open the app, like a bulletin board instead of a doorbell."
4. **Reverses when:** if max group size drops to a few hundred, I delete the read path and go pure write — simpler.
5. **🗣️ How to say it:** "I'd flip the whole thing to fan-out-on-write if you cap groups small — the read path only earns its complexity at broadcast scale."

**🔀 Decision: At-least-once + client dedup vs chasing exactly-once**
1. **Chose & why:** exactly-once is unachievable end-to-end; at-least-once + messageId dedup gives the same user-visible result, simply.
2. **Gave up:** the theoretical purity; consumers must be idempotent.
3. **🗣️ Plain words:** "I might try to deliver twice, but every device ignores a message it's already seen, so you never see a duplicate."
4. **Reverses when:** never — this is the right call at any scale.
5. **🗣️ How to say it:** "I don't pretend to do exactly-once; I do at-least-once and dedupe, which is what every real chat system does."

**🔀 Decision: Stateful WS gateways vs stateless HTTP polling**
1. **Chose & why:** sub-second push requires a held connection.
2. **Gave up:** statelessness — losing a box drops 1M sockets and needs reconnect/routing machinery.
3. **🗣️ Plain words:** "I keep a live phone line open to each device so I can ring them instantly, instead of them calling every few seconds to ask."
4. **Reverses when:** if clients can't hold sockets (locked-down networks) I fall back to long-polling and eat the latency.
5. **🗣️ How to say it:** "Persistent sockets are the only way to get sub-second push; the price is stateful servers and a reconnect story, which I handle with the routing registry and mailbox."

**🔀 Decision: Redis (volatile) for routing/presence vs durable store**
1. **Chose & why:** 7M sub-ms reads/s; data is cheaply reconstructable from reconnects/heartbeats.
2. **Gave up:** durability of routing/presence — acceptable, they self-heal in seconds.
3. **🗣️ Plain words:** "The 'who's online and where' table doesn't need to survive a crash — it rebuilds itself in seconds."
4. **Reverses when:** if reconnect storms after a Redis failure become a thundering-herd problem, I'd add a warm standby.

> **CAP/PACELC:** the message **persist** path is **CP** (we'd rather reject a send than lose ordering/durability under partition). Routing/presence and delivery are **AP** (stay available, tolerate staleness). **PACELC:** under normal operation (no partition) we favor **latency over consistency** on delivery/presence, and **consistency over latency** on the persist/seq path.

> 🎙️ **Script:** "The decisions I'm least sure of: the hybrid fan-out — it's the right call at broadcast scale but it's extra complexity I'd happily delete if groups stay small; and choosing volatile Redis for routing, which is fine as long as reconnect storms don't bite. The persist path is strictly CP — I'll reject a send before I'll lose a message's order — while delivery and presence are AP and favor latency. The whole design traces back to one principle: never lose an acked message; everything else can degrade."

## 11. Likely Interviewer Questions & Answers

**❓ Two people send to the same conversation at the exact same time — what order do they show in?**
- **Mechanism:** both sends hit the single conversation-owner shard, which assigns sequence numbers atomically — one gets seq N, the other N+1. Clients render strictly by seq, so both see the same order.
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** "There's one stamping machine per chat; whoever it stamps first is first, and everyone sees that same order."
- 💬 "One owner per conversation assigns the sequence, so concurrent sends are totally ordered — never ambiguous."

**❓ How do you find which server a user is connected to?**
- **Mechanism:** on connect, the gateway writes `userId/deviceId → gatewayId` into a sharded Redis routing registry with a heartbeat-refreshed TTL; delivery looks it up and routes one event to that box.
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** "A phonebook says which switchboard you're plugged into right now, so I route straight there."
- 💬 "Routing registry in Redis, sharded by user — one lookup, one routed event, falls through to the mailbox if stale."

**❓ What happens to the message if the recipient is offline?**
- **Mechanism:** delivery writes to the recipient's durable per-device mailbox and triggers a batched APNs/FCM push; on reconnect the device replays from its cursor.
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** "It waits in their inbox and they get it the moment they come back, plus a notification."
- 💬 "Durable mailbox plus a cursor — push is just a nudge, the mailbox is the truth."

**❓ A celebrity posts to a 100K-member group — how doesn't that fall over?**
- **Mechanism:** ingest writes one message + one event; for mega-groups we either chunk the member list across hundreds of fan-out workers (drains in ~1s) or flip to fan-out-on-read (store once, members pull on open).
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** "I don't push to 100K people from one machine — I either split the work across hundreds of workers or let people grab it when they open the chat."
- 💬 "Async chunked fan-out, and past ~10K members, fan-out-on-read — the write storm just disappears."

**❓ What if a WS gateway crashes with a million sockets on it?**
- **Mechanism:** clients detect the dead socket and auto-reconnect to a healthy gateway, which re-registers routing; in-flight deliveries that hit the stale entry fall through to the mailbox.
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** "Everyone on that box reconnects in a few seconds, and nothing's lost because messages were already saved."
- 💬 "Reconnect + re-register; the mailbox covers the gap. Blast radius is half a percent of the fleet."

**❓ What if Redis (routing/presence) goes down?**
- **Mechanism:** routing falls through to the mailbox (delivered slightly late); presence just goes stale (dots freeze); both rebuild from reconnects/heartbeats within seconds — neither is durable by design.
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** "Messages arrive a beat late and the online dots freeze, but nothing breaks — it heals itself."
- 💬 "Routing and presence are volatile on purpose — they self-heal from reconnects in seconds."

**❓ Can the same message be delivered twice or show up twice?**
- **Mechanism:** at-least-once delivery, but every device dedups on the client-generated messageId, and the server upserts on it — a retry returns the original seq with no second fan-out.
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** "We might try twice, but your phone ignores a message it's already seen."
- 💬 "At-least-once plus messageId dedup — the user never sees a duplicate."

**❓ Can messages arrive out of order?**
- **Mechanism:** per-conversation sequence numbers + client renders by seq + brief gap buffering; receipts are ordered against the same seq.
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** "Each chat has its own counter, so messages always sort correctly even if the network jumbles them."
- 💬 "Per-conversation seq is the source of truth for order; clients just sort by it."

**❓ How do you stop someone forging the sender or faking 'read' receipts?**
- **Mechanism:** the caller identity comes from the JWT, never a client field; senderId and seq are server-set; receipts are authorized by membership (object-level authz checks the caller is in the conversation).
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** "You can only act as yourself and only in chats you're actually in — the server decides who you are, not the request."
- 💬 "Identity from the JWT, sender/seq server-set, membership-checked on every op — nothing forgeable."

**❓ How is end-to-end encryption affecting your server design?**
- **Mechanism:** the server stores and routes ciphertext only; it never decrypts, so there's no content index, no server-side search, and erasure is crypto-shredding the key.
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** "The server moves sealed envelopes — it never opens them, so it can't search or read your chats."
- 💬 "Server is a dumb pipe for ciphertext — simpler, but it gives up search and moderation."

**❓ What's the most expensive part, and how would you cut it?**
- **Mechanism:** the fan-out + gateway fleet (~$365K of ~$470K/mo); the biggest lever is the fan-out-on-read flip for mega-groups, which removes the dominant write-amplification.
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** "Delivery, not storage, is the cost — and the cheapest fix is to stop pushing to giant groups and let them pull."
- 💬 "Fan-out is the budget; fan-out-on-read for big groups is the single biggest cost lever."

**❓ Presence for a million-follower account — doesn't that storm?**
- **Mechanism:** presence is pull-on-demand for *visible* contacts only, not broadcast-on-change, so going online never fans out to followers; it's a TTL key read when someone opens your chat.
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** "Going online doesn't ping everyone — people only see your status when they look at your chat."
- 💬 "Presence is subscribe-to-visible, not broadcast — the celebrity online-storm never happens."

**❓ How would you add typing indicators / reactions later?**
- **Mechanism:** typing is an ephemeral, sheddable presence-class event (no persistence, lowest priority under load); reactions are tiny messages on the existing delivery path keyed to a target messageId.
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** "Typing is throwaway like presence; reactions ride the same rails as messages."
- 💬 "Typing reuses the presence channel (sheddable); reactions are just small messages — both fit the existing paths."

**❓ How would you support multi-device sync for one user?**
- **Mechanism:** each device is its own delivery target with its own mailbox and cursor; a sent message fans out to all of the sender's *other* devices too, and read watermarks sync across them.
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** "Your phone and laptop are separate inboxes that both get every message and stay caught up to each other."
- 💬 "Per-device mailboxes + cursors — multi-device is just N delivery targets per user."

**❓ How do you handle a thundering-herd reconnect after a big outage?**
- **Mechanism:** clients use jittered exponential backoff; gateways accept connections with admission control and rate-limit re-registration; the mailbox absorbs the backlog so reconnects can be staggered.
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** "Everyone reconnects with random delays so they don't all hit at once, and their messages are safely waiting."
- 💬 "Jittered backoff + admission control + the mailbox as buffer — the herd spreads out, nothing's lost."

> 🎙️ **60-second verbal summary:** "This is WhatsApp-scale chat — every message lands on every device, in order, in under a second, even when you're offline. The design splits into two halves. The delivery half is a fleet of stateful WebSocket gateways holding two hundred million live sockets, with a sharded routing registry in Redis that knows which gateway holds you, so a message routes to exactly one box and gets pushed down your socket — and a durable per-device mailbox catches anything for offline users and replays it on reconnect. The write-and-order half is an ingest tier sharded by conversation that stamps a per-conversation sequence number and persists the message with a quorum write before we ever ack, so ordering is exact and acked messages are never lost; the hardest problem there is the hundred-thousand-member group, which I solve with chunked async fan-out plus a fan-out-on-read flip so one tap doesn't become a write storm. The two halves connect through a partitioned delivery bus that decouples ingest speed from delivery and lets me shed presence and receipts first under overload. Every hard decision traces back to one principle: never lose an acked message — everything else is allowed to degrade."
